question: On a midsagittal perianal ultrasound, which muscular layer lies immediately internal to the external anal sphincter?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the midsagittal perianal ultrasound image and note the labeled structures: the external anal sphincter (EAS) is shown more superficially, the internal anal sphincter (IAS) is shown immediately medial to it, and the mucosa is labeled as the most internal layer.

Next, I relate these visible labels to the question, which asks specifically which muscular layer lies immediately internal to the EAS.

Then, I compare the options to what is seen: option A (Internal anal sphincter) corresponds to the structure labeled IAS that is directly internal to the EAS on the image. Option B (Puborectalis muscle) is not labeled on this midsagittal image and is not shown immediately adjacent to the EAS here. Option D (Rectal mucosa) is labeled as the innermost layer on the image but is not the layer immediately internal to the EAS; it lies deeper than the IAS. Option C (Submucosa) is not depicted or labeled in this image as the layer immediately internal to the EAS.

Finally, tying the observations together: the image clearly shows the IAS positioned immediately internal to the EAS, so the internal anal sphincter is the muscular layer directly internal to the external anal sphincter.
